Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING THROUGH STOCHASTIC DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
**Yang Song**[˚] **Jascha Sohl-Dickstein** Stanford University Google Brain yangsong@cs.stanford.edu jaschasd@google.com

**Diederik P. Kingma** Google Brain durk@google.com

**Abhishek Kumar Stefano Ermon Ben Poole** Google Brain Stanford University Google Brain abhishk@google.com ermon@cs.stanford.edu pooleb@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Creating noise from data is easy; creating data from noise is generative modeling. We present a stochastic differential equation (SDE) that smoothly transforms a complex data distribution to a known prior distribution by slowly injecting noise, and a corresponding reverse-time SDE that transforms the prior distribution back into the data distribution by slowly removing the noise. Crucially, the reverse-time SDE depends only on the time-dependent gradient field (a.k.a., score) of the perturbed data distribution. By leveraging advances in score-based generative modeling, we can accurately estimate these scores with neural networks, and use numerical SDE solvers to generate samples. We show that this framework encapsulates previous approaches in score-based generative modeling and diffusion probabilistic modeling, allowing for new sampling procedures and new modeling capabilities. In particular, we introduce a predictor-corrector framework to correct errors in the evolution of the discretized reverse-time SDE. We also derive an equivalent neural ODE that samples from the same distribution as the SDE, but additionally enables exact likelihood computation, and improved sampling efficiency. In addition, we provide a new way to solve inverse problems with score-based models, as demonstrated with experiments on class-conditional generation, image inpainting, and colorization. Combined with multiple architectural improvements, we achieve record-breaking performance for unconditional image generation on CIFAR-10 with an Inception score of 9.89 and FID of 2.20, a competitive likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim, and demonstrate high fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based generative model.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Two successful classes of probabilistic generative models involve sequentially corrupting training data with slowly increasing noise, and then learning to reverse this corruption in order to form a generative model of the data. _Score matching with Langevin dynamics_ (SMLD) (Song & Ermon, 2019) estimates the _score_ ( _i.e_ ., the gradient of the log probability density with respect to data) at each noise scale, and then uses Langevin dynamics to sample from a sequence of decreasing noise scales during generation. _Denoising diffusion probabilistic modeling_ (DDPM) (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) trains a sequence of probabilistic models to reverse each step of the noise corruption, using knowledge of the functional form of the reverse distributions to make training tractable. For continuous state spaces, the DDPM training objective implicitly computes scores at each noise scale. We therefore refer to these two model classes together as _score-based generative models_ .

Score-based generative models, and related techniques (Bordes et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Du & Mordatch, 2019), have proven effective at generation of images (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020; Ho et al., 2020), audio (Chen et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2020), graphs (Niu et al., 2020), and shapes (Cai

et al., 2020). To enable new sampling methods and further extend the capabilities of score-based generative models, we propose a unified framework that generalizes previous approaches through the lens of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).

Specifically, instead of perturbing data with a finite number of noise distributions, we consider a continuum of distributions that evolve over time according to a diffusion process. This process progressively diffuses a data point into random noise, and is given by a prescribed SDE that does not depend on the data and has no trainable parameters. By reversing this process, we can smoothly mold random noise into data for sample generation. Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time. We can therefore approximate the reverse-time SDE by training a time-dependent neural network to estimate the scores, and then produce samples using numerical SDE solvers. Our key idea is summarized in Fig. 1.

Our proposed framework has several theoretical and practical contributions:

**Flexible sampling and likelihood computation:** We can employ any general-purpose SDE solver to integrate the reverse-time SDE for sampling. In addition, we propose two special methods not viable for general SDEs: (i) Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers that combine numerical SDE solvers with score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981) and HMC (Neal et al., 2011); and (ii) deterministic samplers based on the probability flow ordinary differential equation (ODE). The former _unifies and improves_ over existing sampling methods for score-based models. The latter allows for _fast adaptive sampling_ via black-box ODE solvers, _flexible data manipulation_ via latent codes, a _uniquely identifiable encoding_ , and notably, _exact likelihood computation_ .

**Controllable generation:** We can modulate the generation process by conditioning on information not available during training, because the conditional reverse-time SDE can be efficiently estimated from _unconditional_ scores. This enables applications such as class-conditional generation, image inpainting, colorization and other inverse problems, all achievable using a single unconditional score-based model without re-training.

**Unified framework:** Our framework provides a unified way to explore and tune various SDEs for improving score-based generative models. The methods of SMLD and DDPM can be amalgamated into our framework as discretizations of two separate SDEs. Although DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) was recently reported to achieve higher sample quality than SMLD (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), we show that with better architectures and new sampling algorithms allowed by our framework, the latter can catch up—it achieves new state-of-the-art Inception score (9.89) and FID score (2.20) on CIFAR-10, as well as high-fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based model. In addition, we propose a new SDE under our framework that achieves a likelihood value of 2.99 bits/dim on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10 images, setting a new record on this task.

## 3 SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING WITH SDES

Perturbing data with multiple noise scales is key to the success of previous methods. We propose to generalize this idea further to an infinite number of noise scales, such that perturbed data distributions evolve according to an SDE as the noise intensifies. An overview of our framework is given in Fig. 2.

## 4.1 GENERAL-PURPOSE NUMERICAL SDE SOLVERS

Numerical solvers provide approximate trajectories from SDEs. Many general-purpose numerical methods exist for solving SDEs, such as Euler-Maruyama and stochastic Runge-Kutta methods (Kloeden & Platen, 2013), which correspond to different discretizations of the stochastic dynamics. We can apply any of them to the reverse-time SDE for sample generation.

Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E). Deriving the ancestral sampling rules for new SDEs, however, can be non-trivial. To remedy this, we propose _reverse diffusion samplers_ (details in Appendix E), which discretize the reverse-time SDE in the same way as the forward one, and thus can be readily derived given the forward discretization. As shown in Table 1, reverse diffusion samplers perform slightly better than ancestral sampling for both SMLD and DDPM models on CIFAR-10 (DDPM-type ancestral sampling is also applicable to SMLD models, see Appendix F.)

## 4.2 PREDICTOR-CORRECTOR SAMPLERS

Unlike generic SDEs, we have additional information that can be used to improve solutions. Since we have a score-based model **s** _**θ**_ ˚p **x** _, t_ q « _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **x** q, we can employ score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981; Grenander & Miller, 1994) or HMC (Neal et al., 2011) to sample from _pt_ directly, and correct the solution of a numerical SDE solver.

Specifically, at each time step, the numerical SDE solver first gives an estimate of the sample at the next time step, playing the role of a “predictor”. Then, the score-based MCMC approach corrects the marginal distribution of the estimated sample, playing the role of a “corrector”. The idea is analogous to Predictor-Corrector methods, a family of numerical continuation techniques for solving systems of equations (Allgower & Georg, 2012), and we similarly name our hybrid sampling algorithms _Predictor-Corrector_ (PC) samplers. Please find pseudo-code and a complete description in Appendix G. PC samplers generalize the original sampling methods of SMLD and DDPM: the former uses an identity function as the predictor and annealed Langevin dynamics as the corrector, while the latter uses ancestral sampling as the predictor and identity as the corrector.

We test PC samplers on SMLD and DDPM models (see Algorithms 2 and 3 in Appendix G) trained with original discrete objectives given by Eqs. (1) and (3). This exhibits the compatibility of PC samplers to score-based models trained with a fixed number of noise scales. We summarize the performance of different samplers in Table 1, where probability flow is a predictor to be discussed in Section 4.3. Detailed experimental settings and additional results are given in Appendix G. We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling, and corrector-only methods (C2000) perform worse than other competitors (P2000, PC1000) with the same computation (In fact, we need way more corrector steps per noise scale, and thus more computation, to match the performance of other samplers.) For all predictors, adding one corrector step for each predictor step (PC1000) doubles computation but always improves sample quality (against P1000). Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000), where we have to interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner (detailed in Appendix G) for SMLD/DDPM models. ## 4.3 PROBABILITY FLOW AND CONNECTION TO NEURAL ODES

Score-based models enable another numerical method for solving the reverse-time SDE. For all diffusion processes, there exists a corresponding _deterministic process_ whose trajectories share the same marginal probability densities t _pt_ p **x** qu _[T] t_ “0[as the SDE. **Exact likelihood computation** Leveraging the connection to neural ODEs, we can compute the density defined by Eq. (13) via the instantaneous change of variables formula (Chen et al., 2018). This allows us to compute the _exact likelihood on any input data_ (details in Appendix D.2). As an example, we report negative log-likelihoods (NLLs) measured in bits/dim on the CIFAR-10 dataset in Table 2. We compute log-likelihoods on uniformly dequantized data, and only compare to models evaluated in the same way (omitting models evaluated with variational dequantization (Ho et al., 2019) or discrete data), except for DDPM ( _L_ / _L_ simple) whose ELBO values (annotated with *) are reported on discrete data. Main results: (i) For the same DDPM model in Ho et al. (2020), we obtain better bits/dim than ELBO, since our likelihoods are exact; (ii) Using the same architecture, we trained another DDPM model with the continuous objective in Eq. (7) ( _i.e_ ., DDPM cont.), which further improves the likelihood; (iii) With sub-VP SDEs, we always get higher likelihoods compared to VP SDEs; (iv) With improved architecture ( _i.e_ ., DDPM++ cont., details in Section 4.4) and the sub-VP SDE, we can set a new record bits/dim of 2.99 on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10 even _without maximum likelihood training_ .

**Manipulating latent representations** By integrating Eq. (13), we can encode any datapoint **x** p0q into a latent space **x** p _T_ q. Decoding can be achieved by integrating a corresponding ODE for the reverse-time SDE. As is done with other invertible models such as neural ODEs and normalizing flows (Dinh et al., 2016; Kingma & Dhariwal, 2018), we can manipulate this latent representation for image editing, such as interpolation, and temperature scaling (see Fig. 3 and Appendix D.4).

**Uniquely identifiable encoding** Unlike most current invertible models, our encoding is _uniquely identifiable_ , meaning that with sufficient training data, model capacity, and optimization accuracy, the encoding for an input is uniquely determined by the data distribution (Roeder et al., 2020). ## 4.4 ARCHITECTURE IMPROVEMENTS

We explore several new architecture designs for score-based models using both VE and VP SDEs (details in Appendix H), where we train models with the same discrete objectives as in SMLD/DDPM. We directly transfer the architectures for VP SDEs to sub-VP SDEs due to their similarity. Our optimal architecture for the VE SDE, named NCSN++, achieves an FID of 2.45 on CIFAR-10 with PC samplers, while our optimal architecture for the VP SDE, called DDPM++, achieves 2.78.

By switching to the continuous training objective in Eq. (7), and increasing the network depth, we can further improve sample quality for all models. The resulting architectures are denoted as NCSN++ cont. and DDPM++ cont. in Table 3 for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively. Results reported in Table 3 are for the checkpoint with the smallest FID over the course of training, where samples are generated with PC samplers. In contrast, FID scores and NLL values in Table 2 are reported for the last training checkpoint, and samples are obtained with black-box ODE solvers. As shown in Table 3, VE SDEs typically provide better sample quality than VP/sub-VP SDEs, but we also empirically observe that their likelihoods are worse than VP/sub-VP SDE counterparts. This indicates that practitioners likely need to experiment with different SDEs for varying domains and architectures.

Our best model for sample quality, NCSN++ cont. (deep, VE), doubles the network depth and sets new records for both inception score and FID on unconditional generation for CIFAR-10. Surprisingly, we can achieve better FID than the previous best conditional generative model without requiring labeled data. With all improvements together, we also obtain the first set of high-fidelity samples on CelebA-HQ 1024 ˆ 1024 from score-based models (see Appendix H.3). Our best model for likelihoods, DDPM++ cont. (deep, sub-VP), similarly doubles the network depth and achieves a log-likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim with the continuous objective in Eq. (7). To our best knowledge, this is the highest likelihood on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10.

In some cases, it is possible to train a separate model to learn the forward process log _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq and compute its gradient. In Appendix I.4, we provide a broadly applicable method for obtaining such an estimate without the need of training auxiliary models.

We consider three applications of controllable generation with this approach: class-conditional generation, image imputation and colorization. When **y** represents class labels, we can train a time-dependent classifier _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq for class-conditional sampling. Since the forward SDE is tractable, we can easily create training data p **x** p _t_ q _,_ **y** q for the time-dependent classifier by first sampling p **x** p0q _,_ **y** q from a dataset, and then sampling **x** p _t_ q „ _p_ 0 _t_ p **x** p _t_ q | **x** p0qq. Afterwards, we may employ a mixture of cross-entropy losses over different time steps, like Eq. (7), to train the time-dependent classifier _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq. We provide class-conditional CIFAR-10 samples in Fig. 4 (left), and relegate more details and results to Appendix I.

Imputation is a special case of conditional sampling. Suppose we have an incomplete data point **y** where only some subset, Ωp **y** q is known. Imputation amounts to sampling from _p_ p **x** p0q | Ωp **y** qq, which we can accomplish using an unconditional model (see Appendix I.2). Colorization is a special case of imputation, except that the known data dimensions are coupled. We can decouple these data dimensions with an orthogonal linear transformation, and perform imputation in the transformed space (details in Appendix I.3). Fig. 4 (right) shows results for inpainting and colorization achieved with unconditional time-dependent score-based models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We presented a framework for score-based generative modeling based on SDEs. Our work enables a better understanding of existing approaches, new sampling algorithms, exact likelihood computation, uniquely identifiable encoding, latent code manipulation, and brings new conditional generation abilities to the family of score-based generative models.

While our proposed sampling approaches improve results and enable more efficient sampling, they remain slower at sampling than GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014) on the same datasets. Identifying ways of combining the stable learning of score-based generative models with the fast sampling of implicit models like GANs remains an important research direction. Additionally, the breadth of samplers one can use when given access to score functions introduces a number of hyper-parameters. Future work would benefit from improved methods to automatically select and tune these hyperparameters, as well as more extensive investigation on the merits and limitations of various samplers.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents a highly significant contribution to generative modeling, unifying score-based generative models (SMLD) and denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM) under a common continuous-time framework based on stochastic differential equations. The framing is elegant and conceptually powerful: by viewing noise corruption as a forward SDE and generation as its reverse-time counterpart (requiring only the score function), the authors elucidate deep connections between previously disparate methods while unlocking new capabilities—predictor-corrector samplers, a probability-flow ODE enabling exact likelihoods and fast sampling, and controllable/conditional generation via unconditional scores. The empirical results are impressive, with state-of-the-art CIFAR-10 Inception (9.89) and FID (2.20) scores, competitive likelihoods (2.99 bits/dim), and the first high-fidelity 1024×1024 samples from a score-based model. The writing is clear, the abstract and introduction are well-structured, and Figure 1 conveys the core idea intuitively. The main concerns a reviewer should probe further in the body of the paper are: (1) whether the theoretical contributions (reverse-time SDE, probability flow ODE) are genuinely novel or primarily adaptations of known results from Anderson (1982) and related stochastic analysis literature, with the novelty residing in the synthesis and application; (2) the breadth of the contribution list risks diluting focus, and readers may benefit from a sharper statement of which result is the paper's single most important claim; (3) practical trade-offs—computational cost of SDE/ODE solvers vs. discrete samplers, sensitivity to SDE choice, and stability of training time-dependent score networks—should be carefully substantiated; and (4) the claim that controllable generation works "without re-training" via unconditional scores should be rigorously justified, since conditional score estimation typically requires additional assumptions or approximations. Overall, this appears to be a landmark contribution whose clarity and results justify strong acceptance, pending scrutiny of the novelty boundaries with prior diffusion and score-matching literature.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is exceptionally strong in motivation, positioning, and conceptual clarity: it identifies a unifying continuous-time perspective on score-based models and diffusion probabilistic models, explains the central reverse-time SDE idea succinctly, and clearly articulates why this framework enables new sampling, likelihood, and conditional-generation capabilities. The abstract and introduction are compelling and well organized, with contributions stated at both theoretical and practical levels, and the connection to prior SMLD/DDPM work is framed in a way that makes the paper’s novelty immediately apparent. The most significant issues are likely not in presentation but in technical accessibility and substantiation: the beginning makes several broad claims—exact likelihood computation, improved sampling efficiency, controllable generation from unconditional scores, and state-of-the-art empirical performance—that will require careful assumptions, derivations, and ablations later in the paper to be fully convincing. Some readers may also find the transition from discrete noise schedules to continuous SDEs mathematically dense, especially given the notation corruption in the provided text, so the paper’s clarity depends heavily on whether subsequent sections rigorously but accessibly define the reverse SDE, probability-flow ODE, and training objectives. Overall, this appears to be a high-quality and potentially field-shaping paper, with the main risks being the complexity of the framework, the need to disentangle gains from architectural versus algorithmic changes, and the importance of clearly specifying the conditions under which the proposed likelihood and inverse-problem methods are valid.

comments.title: Terminal distribution is presented as an exact fixed prior, though examples generally only approximate this
comments.quote: Typically, _pT_ is an unstructured prior distribution that contains no information of _p_ 0, such as a Gaussian distribution with fixed mean and variance. There are various ways of designing the SDE in Eq. (5) such that it diffuses the data distribution into a fixed prior distribution.
comments.explanation: The intended picture is clear: choose a forward noising SDE whose terminal marginal is simple enough to sample from, then run the reverse dynamics from that terminal law. Earlier in the passage the authors are careful about this issue for SMLD and DDPM, saying, for example, that \(p_{\sigma_{\max}}\) and \(x_N\) are only approximately Gaussian. What remains slightly problematic is that the general SDE formulation here sounds exact: it suggests that at a finite terminal time \(T\), the marginal \(p_T\) is a fixed prior distribution containing no information about \(p_0\). For common finite-time VE/VP constructions, the terminal law is still the forward marginal induced by the data distribution—e.g. a convolution or attenuated mixture—and is only close to a Gaussian when the noise level is large enough or the signal coefficient is sufficiently small. A careful reader could therefore wonder whether subsequent sampling and likelihood claims are exact with respect to the true terminal marginal or approximate because the sampler is initialized from a convenient Gaussian prior. This can be fixed by explicitly saying that in many practical SDEs the terminal marginal is chosen to be approximately equal to a tractable prior, unless the schedule is designed so that equality holds in a limiting or exact sense.

comments.title: Conditional generation requires auxiliary conditional information and terminal initialization, not unconditional scores alone
comments.quote: We can modulate the generation process by conditioning on information not available during training, because the conditional reverse-time SDE can be efficiently estimated from _unconditional_ scores.
comments.explanation: The intended contribution is defensible: a single unconditional score model can be reused for multiple conditional tasks without retraining that score model. However, the conditional reverse SDE is not determined by the unconditional score alone. It also requires the conditional score term \(\nabla_x \log p_t(y\mid x(t))\), which must come from a classifier, observation model, inverse-problem likelihood, learned auxiliary model, or approximation. In inverse problems, this term is also not simply the usual clean-data forward model \(p(y\mid x(0))\); it is the time-marginal conditional \(p_t(y\mid x(t))\) induced jointly by the data distribution, observation model, and SDE. Exact conditional sampling further requires initialization from \(p_T(x(T)\mid y)\), or an approximation such as treating \(x(T)\) as nearly independent of \(y\) when the terminal distribution is close to pure noise. Thus the text slightly overstates what follows from an unconditional score model by itself.

comments.title: Variance explosion claim needs an unbounded-noise assumption
comments.quote: Interestingly, the SDE of Eq. (9) always gives a process with exploding variance when _t_ Ñ 8, whilst the SDE of Eq. (11) yields a process with a fixed variance of one when the initial distribution has unit variance (proof in Appendix B).
comments.explanation: The intended contrast between the VE and VP SDEs is clear and standard: for the VE SDE derived from SMLD, the conditional variance accumulates according to the chosen noise schedule, whereas the VP SDE has an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck-like drift/diffusion balance that preserves unit marginal variance when initialized with unit variance. However, the word “always” is too strong as stated. For the VE SDE, the variance is essentially Var[x(t)] = Var[x(0)] + σ²(t) - σ²(0) under the usual formulation dx = sqrt(dσ²(t)/dt) dw. It explodes as t → ∞ only if the schedule σ(t) itself is unbounded. In the paper’s own setup, the diffusion is usually run on a finite time interval, and SMLD uses a finite σ_max. Thus the name “Variance Exploding” is appropriate for the intended unbounded or increasing-noise family, but the mathematical claim requires an assumption such as σ(t) → ∞ as t → ∞. Without that qualifier, bounded increasing schedules would be counterexamples.

comments.title: DDPM likelihood comparison conflates ELBO, data domains, and the ODE-induced model
comments.quote: For the same DDPM model in Ho et al. (2020), we obtain better bits/dim than ELBO, since our likelihoods are exact;
comments.explanation: This is a reasonable high-level contrast between an exact change-of-variables likelihood for the probability-flow ODE and the variational bound reported for DDPMs, but the comparison needs more qualification. The surrounding text acknowledges that the authors compute likelihoods on uniformly dequantized data, while the DDPM ELBO values are reported on discrete data and marked with an asterisk. That helps, but it does not fully resolve the issue. The likelihood computed via the probability-flow ODE is the exact likelihood of the continuous ODE model induced by the learned score field, not obviously the exact likelihood of the original finite-step DDPM reverse Markov chain used by Ho et al. unless an additional equivalence argument is made. Also, the improvement over an ELBO is not solely explained by being “exact”: the table compares an exact ODE likelihood on uniformly dequantized inputs against variational upper bounds reported for a discrete-data DDPM objective. A reader could therefore come away thinking that the original Ho et al. DDPM itself has an exact NLL of 3.28 bits/dim, whereas the result appears to be for a related probability-flow or continuous interpretation using the DDPM score network. The claim would be safer if it explicitly distinguished the model whose likelihood is being evaluated and separated the effects of exact likelihood computation from the different evaluation setup.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is exceptionally strong in motivation, positioning, and conceptual clarity: it identifies a unifying continuous-time perspective on score-based models and diffusion probabilistic models, explains the central reverse-time SDE idea succinctly, and clearly articulates why this framework enables new sampling, likelihood, and conditional-generation capabilities. The abstract and introduction are compelling and well organized, with contributions stated at both theoretical and practical levels, and the connection to prior SMLD/DDPM work is framed in a way that makes the paper’s novelty immediately apparent. The most significant issues are likely not in presentation but in technical accessibility and substantiation: the beginning makes several broad claims—exact likelihood computation, improved sampling efficiency, controllable generation from unconditional scores, and state-of-the-art empirical performance—that will require careful assumptions, derivations, and ablations later in the paper to be fully convincing. Some readers may also find the transition from discrete noise schedules to continuous SDEs mathematically dense, especially given the notation corruption in the provided text, so the paper’s clarity depends heavily on whether subsequent sections rigorously but accessibly define the reverse SDE, probability-flow ODE, and training objectives. Overall, this appears to be a high-quality and potentially field-shaping paper, with the main risks being the complexity of the framework, the need to disentangle gains from architectural versus algorithmic changes, and the importance of clearly specifying the conditions under which the proposed likelihood and inverse-problem methods are valid.

comments.title: Endpoint prior is described as exact although finite-time perturbations are generally only approximately prior
comments.quote: We present a stochastic differential equation (SDE) that smoothly transforms a complex data distribution to a known prior distribution by slowly injecting noise, and a corresponding reverse-time SDE that transforms the prior distribution back into the data distribution by slowly removing the noise.
comments.explanation: The intended high-level picture is clear and standard: choose a forward noising SDE whose terminal distribution is close to a simple Gaussian prior, then simulate the reverse process. Later context partly resolves this by saying, for example, that the largest SMLD noise scale is chosen so that the perturbed distribution is approximately Gaussian, and by framing p_T as a tractable prior. However, for the common finite-time VE/VP constructions, the terminal law is not generally exactly a fixed data-independent prior unless one takes an idealized limit or imposes special conditions on the initial distribution. At finite T it is typically only close to the chosen prior. Because exact reversal from the true p_T versus sampling from an approximate Gaussian prior affects the formal generative claim, the unqualified wording “transforms ... to a known prior distribution” slightly overstates what is guaranteed.

comments.title: Conditional generation claim may suggest unconditional scores are sufficient by themselves
comments.quote: We can modulate the generation process by conditioning on information not available during training, because the conditional reverse-time SDE can be efficiently estimated from _unconditional_ scores.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contribution: the same unconditional score model can be reused for multiple conditional tasks, avoiding retraining the generative model. In the standard derivation, however, the conditional score is not obtained from the unconditional score alone; one needs an additional term such as ∇_x log p_t(y|x) from a classifier, observation model, or inverse-problem likelihood, often with approximations. The following sentence’s “single unconditional score-based model without re-training” is defensible if it means without retraining the score model, but the quoted causal statement could mislead a reader into thinking the unconditional score determines the conditional reverse SDE without any auxiliary conditional information or model.

comments.title: “Exact likelihood computation” is stated without numerical/model-approximation qualifications
comments.quote: We also derive an equivalent neural ODE that samples from the same distribution as the SDE, but additionally enables exact likelihood computation, and improved sampling efficiency.
comments.explanation: The underlying idea is correct: the probability-flow ODE has the same marginal distributions as the corresponding SDE under the exact score, and the continuous normalizing-flow change-of-variables formula gives a likelihood rather than merely a variational bound. Still, the statement is stronger than what is literally available in practice. With a learned approximate score, numerical ODE integration, and usually numerical or stochastic trace/divergence estimation, likelihoods are exact only for the specified approximate ODE up to solver and estimator error, not exact for the data distribution or the ideal reverse SDE. This is a common shorthand in the neural ODE/CNF literature, but a qualifier would avoid overreading.

comments.title: Terminal distribution is presented as an exact fixed prior, though examples generally only approximate this
comments.quote: Typically, _pT_ is an unstructured prior distribution that contains no information of _p_ 0, such as a Gaussian distribution with fixed mean and variance. There are various ways of designing the SDE in Eq. (5) such that it diffuses the data distribution into a fixed prior distribution.
comments.explanation: The intended picture is clear: choose a forward noising SDE whose terminal marginal is simple enough to sample from, then run the reverse dynamics from that terminal law. Earlier in the passage the authors are careful about this issue for SMLD and DDPM, saying, for example, that \(p_{\sigma_{\max}}\) and \(x_N\) are only approximately Gaussian. What remains slightly problematic is that the general SDE formulation here sounds exact: it suggests that at a finite terminal time \(T\), the marginal \(p_T\) is a fixed prior distribution containing no information about \(p_0\). For common finite-time VE/VP constructions, the terminal law is still the forward marginal induced by the data distribution—e.g. a convolution or attenuated mixture—and is only close to a Gaussian when the noise level is large enough or the signal coefficient is sufficiently small. A careful reader could therefore wonder whether subsequent sampling and likelihood claims are exact with respect to the true terminal marginal or approximate because the sampler is initialized from a convenient Gaussian prior. This can be fixed by explicitly saying that in many practical SDEs the terminal marginal is chosen to be approximately equal to a tractable prior, unless the schedule is designed so that equality holds in a limiting or exact sense.

comments.title: Reverse-time diffusion statement is broader than the stated assumptions justify
comments.quote: A remarkable result from Anderson (1982) states that the reverse of a diffusion process is also a diffusion process, running backwards in time and given by the reverse-time SDE:
comments.explanation: The authors are invoking a standard and central result, and in the immediately surrounding context they restrict attention to scalar, state-independent diffusion coefficients \(g(t)\), for which the familiar reverse drift involving \(-g(t)^2\nabla_x \log p_t(x)\) is the relevant formula. They also cite Anderson, so this is not an unsupported claim. Still, the prose is somewhat too broad as written. Time reversal of diffusions requires regularity assumptions on the process and its marginal densities, and the simple reverse-SDE form used here depends on the diffusion coefficient being state-independent; for state-dependent diffusion matrices, additional divergence terms appear in the reverse drift under the usual Haussmann–Pardoux/Anderson formulations. The paper notes elsewhere that generalizations are in the appendix, which mitigates the concern, but the local statement could mislead a reader into thinking the displayed reverse SDE applies unchanged to arbitrary diffusion processes.

comments.title: Variance explosion claim needs an unbounded-noise assumption
comments.quote: Interestingly, the SDE of Eq. (9) always gives a process with exploding variance when _t_ Ñ 8, whilst the SDE of Eq. (11) yields a process with a fixed variance of one when the initial distribution has unit variance (proof in Appendix B).
comments.explanation: The intended contrast between the VE and VP SDEs is clear and standard: for the VE SDE derived from SMLD, the conditional variance accumulates according to the chosen noise schedule, whereas the VP SDE has an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck-like drift/diffusion balance that preserves unit marginal variance when initialized with unit variance. However, the word “always” is too strong as stated. For the VE SDE, the variance is essentially Var[x(t)] = Var[x(0)] + σ^2(t) - σ^2(0) under the usual formulation dx = sqrt(dσ^2(t)/dt) dw. It explodes as t → ∞ only if the schedule σ(t) itself is unbounded. In the paper’s own setup, the diffusion is usually run on a finite time interval, and SMLD uses a finite σ_max. Thus the name “Variance Exploding” is appropriate for the intended unbounded or increasing-noise family, but the mathematical claim requires an assumption such as σ(t) → ∞ as t → ∞. Without that qualifier, bounded increasing schedules would be counterexamples.

comments.title: DDPM likelihood comparison conflates ELBO, data domains, and the ODE-induced model
comments.quote: For the same DDPM model in Ho et al. (2020), we obtain better bits/dim than ELBO, since our likelihoods are exact;
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a reasonable contrast between an exact change-of-variables likelihood for the probability-flow ODE and the variational bound reported for DDPMs. The surrounding text does acknowledge an important caveat: the authors compute likelihoods on uniformly dequantized data, while the DDPM \(L\)/\(L_{\text{simple}}\) ELBO values are reported on discrete data and marked with an asterisk. That helps, but it does not fully resolve the comparison. The likelihood computed via the probability-flow ODE is the exact likelihood of the continuous ODE model induced by the learned score field, not obviously the exact likelihood of the original finite-step DDPM reverse Markov chain used by Ho et al. unless an additional equivalence argument is made. Also, the improvement over an ELBO is not solely explained by being “exact”: the table is comparing an exact ODE likelihood on uniformly dequantized inputs against variational upper bounds reported for a discrete-data DDPM objective. A careful reader could therefore come away thinking that the original Ho et al. DDPM itself has an exact NLL of 3.28 bits/dim, whereas the result appears to be for a related probability-flow/continuous interpretation using the DDPM score network. The claim would be safer if it explicitly distinguished the model whose likelihood is being evaluated and separated the effects of exact likelihood computation from the different evaluation setup.

comments.title: Conditional reverse SDE also requires a tractable terminal conditional distribution
comments.quote:
The continuous structure of our framework allows us to not only produce data samples from _p_ 0, but also from _p_ 0p **x** p0q | **y** q if _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq is known. Given a forward SDE as in Eq. (5), we can sample

from _pt_ p **x** p _t_ q | **y** q by starting from _pT_ p **x** p _T_ q | **y** q and solving a conditional reverse-time SDE:
comments.explanation:
The conditional reverse-time drift itself is consistent with the standard time-reversal formula: replacing the unconditional score by \(
abla_x \log p_t(x\mid y)=
abla_x\log p_t(x)+
abla_x\log p_t(y\mid x)\) gives Eq. (14). What initially concerned me is the sufficiency condition stated in prose. Knowing \(p_t(y\mid x(t))\), or even its gradient, is not by itself enough to implement exact conditional sampling unless one can also initialize from \(p_T(x(T)\mid y)\). The next sentence does mention this terminal conditional distribution, which partly resolves the issue, but it leaves open how it is sampled. In many score-based applications one approximates \(p_T(x_T\mid y)\approx p_T(x_T)\) because the terminal distribution is nearly pure noise and nearly independent of \(y\), but that is an additional approximation/assumption. Alternatively, if \(p_T(y\mid x_T)\) and \(p_T(x_T)\) are known, Bayes gives the density up to normalization, but direct sampling can still be nontrivial. The text therefore slightly overstates the condition under which the procedure is directly available.

comments.title: The quantity \(p_t(y\mid x(t))\) is not just the usual inverse-problem forward model
comments.quote: In general, we can use Eq. (14) to solve a large family of _inverse problems_ with score-based generative models, once given an estimate of the gradient of the forward process, _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **y** | **x** p _t_ qq.
comments.explanation: The authors are correct that, if the conditional score term \(\nabla_x\log p_t(y\mid x(t))\) is available, the conditional reverse SDE follows naturally. The potentially misleading point is the phrase “gradient of the forward process.” In inverse problems, the known forward/measurement model is typically \(p(y\mid x(0))\), e.g. masking, blurring, or grayscale projection applied to the clean image. The term needed in Eq. (14) is instead \(p_t(y\mid x(t))\), which generally involves marginalizing over clean data compatible with the noisy state, e.g. \(\int p(y\mid x_0)p(x_0\mid x_t)dx_0\), and depends on the data distribution and the SDE. The following sentences acknowledge that one may need a separate learned model or heuristics, so the authors are not claiming this term is automatically known. Still, a reader could incorrectly infer that specifying the inverse-problem degradation operator alone supplies the required gradient exactly. A brief clarification that \(p_t(y\mid x(t))\) is a time-marginal conditional induced jointly by the data distribution, observation model, and SDE would avoid this ambiguity.

comments.title: Bits/dim is described as log-likelihood although it is conventionally negative log-likelihood
comments.quote: Our best model for likelihoods, DDPM++ cont. (deep, sub-VP), similarly doubles the network depth and achieves a log-likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim with the continuous objective in Eq. (7). To our best knowledge, this is the highest likelihood on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The surrounding context mostly resolves the intended meaning: just before this, the passage refers to “NLL values in Table 2,” and in generative modeling on images, “bits/dim” conventionally denotes negative log-likelihood per dimension, where lower is better. However, the quoted sentence calls 2.99 bits/dim a “log-likelihood” and then says it is the “highest likelihood.” Strictly, the log-likelihood itself would not be reported as a positive bits/dim value in this convention; 2.99 bits/dim is an NLL/bpd value corresponding to a higher likelihood than larger bpd values. This is a common shorthand in the literature, but it is technically imprecise and could confuse readers comparing likelihood numbers, especially because “highest” and “bits/dim” optimize in opposite directions unless one remembers the NLL convention.
